title: Tomato choka
subtitle: Trinidadian fire-roasted tomato with garlic and scotch bonnet
excerpt: Tomatoes and garlic roasted directly over a flame or under a grill until charred and collapsing, then mashed with scotch bonnet and oil. A Trinidadian breakfast side with a deep, smoky edge.
sourceNotes: Choka is a Trinidadian technique of roasting vegetables (commonly tomato, aubergine, or potato) directly over a flame or under a grill until charred, then mashing them with garlic and scotch bonnet. The word 'choka' derives from Hindi/Bhojpuri, reflecting the Indo-Trinidadian origin of the technique. Tomato choka is the most common version and is standard at the Trinidadian breakfast table alongside fry bake and buljol. The charring is essential to the flavour — an oven-roasted tomato is not the same dish.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in the fridge for 2 days. The flavour deepens overnight.
recipe.temperatureNote: direct flame or very hot grill

content.text: Tomato choka is one of those dishes where almost all the flavour comes from a single technique: the direct charring. Tomatoes placed directly on a gas burner or held very close to a grill flame collapse in about 10 minutes, the skin blackening and the interior turning to a sweet, smoky pulp. The garlic, roasted in the same way, becomes soft and slightly bitter at the edges. Together with scotch bonnet and a thread of oil, they make something entirely different from a cooked tomato sauce.

Use the most flavourful tomatoes you can find — larger, imperfect ones are better than perfect round supermarket tomatoes. The charred skin is discarded, but its flavour has already entered the flesh during the roasting.

items.prepNote: finely chopped, or more to taste

content.text: Place the {{tomato}} tomatoes directly on a gas burner set to medium-high, or under a very hot grill. Roast for 10–12 minutes, turning with tongs every 2–3 minutes, until the skin is blackened all over and the flesh is collapsing and completely soft. Place the unpeeled {{garlic}} of garlic cloves on the burner or grill alongside the tomatoes for the last 5 minutes, until the skins are charred and the flesh inside is soft. Leave to cool for 5 minutes.

Peel off and discard the charred tomato skins: they should slip off easily. Squeeze the roasted garlic from its skins. Mash the tomato flesh and garlic together roughly with a fork — choka should be coarse and chunky, not smooth. Stir in the {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet, {{vegetable-oil}} of oil, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Taste and adjust the seasoning.

items.cause: Grill not hot enough; tomatoes too large
items.fix: The grill must be very hot — the skin should blacken within 2–3 minutes per side. Halving very large tomatoes speeds up the charring.

items.cause: Too much liquid released during charring
items.fix: After peeling, hold each tomato briefly over the sink to let excess juice drain. The choka should be thick enough to heap on bread.

items.cause: Used oven rather than direct flame or grill
items.fix: Direct flame or a very hot grill is essential. An oven produces a roasted tomato, not a charred one. The smoke flavour can't be added after the fact.
The garlic should be completely soft inside the skin — it needs at least 5 minutes of direct heat. If the flesh is still firm, continue on the heat.

content.text: Tomato choka is a breakfast condiment and side in Trinidad, eaten spread on fry bake or sada roti alongside other components: buljol, fried egg, or avocado. It is one of the choka family of fire-roasted mashes — aubergine choka (baigan choka) and potato choka (aloo choka) follow the same technique. The method is rooted in the cooking of the Indo-Trinidadian community, a direct descendant of Indian cooking traditions adapted over generations to the ingredients and fuel of the Caribbean.
